([/ˌælɪˈɡeɪni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*AL-ih-GAY-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))—also spelled **Alleghany** or **Allegany**, less formally the **Alleghenies**—is part of the vast [Appalachian Mountain Range](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") of the [Eastern United States](/wiki/Eastern_United_States "Eastern United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). Historically, it represented a significant barrier to westward land travel and development. The Alleghenies have a northeast–southwest orientation, running for about 300 miles (480 km) from north-central [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") southward, through western [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") and eastern [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia").

The **Allegheny Mountain Range** ([/ˌælɪˈɡeɪni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*AL-ih-GAY-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))—also spelled **Alleghany** or **Allegany**, less formally the **Alleghenies**—is part of the vast [Appalachian Mountain Range](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") of the [Eastern United States](/wiki/Eastern_United_States "Eastern United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). Historically, it represented a significant barrier to westward land travel and development. The Alleghenies have a northeast–southwest orientation, running for about 300 miles (480 km) from north-central [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") southward, through western [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") and eastern [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia").

The Alleghenies comprise the rugged western-central portion of the [Appalachians](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"). They rise to 4,862 feet (1,482 m) in northeastern West Virginia. In the east, they are dominated by a high, steep [escarpment](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment") known as the [Allegheny Front](/wiki/Allegheny_Front "Allegheny Front"). In the west, they slope down into the closely associated [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"), which extends into [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") and [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"). The principal settlements of the Alleghenies are [Altoona](/wiki/Altoona,_Pennsylvania "Altoona, Pennsylvania"), [State College](/wiki/State_College,_Pennsylvania "State College, Pennsylvania"), and [Johnstown, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Johnstown,_Pennsylvania "Johnstown, Pennsylvania"); and [Cumberland, Maryland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Maryland "Cumberland, Maryland").

Using the [United States Geological Survey](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") (USGS) classification of physical geography ([physiography](/wiki/Physical_geography "Physical geography")), the Allegheny Mountain range is part of the [Appalachian Plateau](/wiki/Appalachian_Plateau "Appalachian Plateau") province of the [Appalachian Highlands](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") physiographic division.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name is derived from the [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River"), which drains only a small portion of the Alleghenies in west-central Pennsylvania. The meaning of the word, which comes from the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") (Delaware) Native Americans, is not definitively known but is usually translated as "fine river". The closest approximation which makes sense is some context from the Jesuit Relations [[1]](#cite_note-1) showing that Alligeh was one of several accepted renderings of the name of the Erie people among the early 17th century missionaries among the Native peoples throughout the eastern Great Lakes region, along with Rique, Yenresh and Erichronon. The suffix -ni means "of the", in Lenape, despite that geh is also Iroquoian for "of the". So, most likely, Alligehni or Oligini would be the Lenape name for the original homeland of the Erie people.

The word "Allegheny" was once commonly used to refer to the whole of what are now called the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"). [John Norton](/wiki/John_Norton_(Mohawk_chief) "John Norton (Mohawk chief)") used it (spelled variously) around 1810 to refer to the mountains in [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee") and [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)").[[2]](#cite_note-Norton-2) Around the same time, [Washington Irving](/wiki/Washington_Irving "Washington Irving") proposed renaming the United States either "Appalachia" or "Alleghania".[[3]](#cite_note-Stewart-3) In 1861, [Arnold Henry Guyot](/wiki/Arnold_Henry_Guyot "Arnold Henry Guyot") published the first systematic geologic study of the whole mountain range.[[4]](#cite_note-4) His map labeled the range as the "Alleghanies", but his book was titled *On the Appalachian Mountain System*. As late as 1867, [John Muir](/wiki/John_Muir "John Muir")—in his book *A Thousand Mile Walk to the Gulf*—used the word "Alleghanies" in referring to the *southern* Appalachians.

There was no general agreement about the "Appalachians" versus the "Alleghanies" until the late 19th century.[[3]](#cite_note-Stewart-3)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

See also: [List of mountains of the Alleghenies](/wiki/List_of_mountains_of_the_Alleghenies "List of mountains of the Alleghenies")

### Extent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Extent")]

From northeast to southwest, the Allegheny Mountains run about 300 miles (480 km). From west to east, at their widest, they are about 100 miles (160 km).[[a]](#cite_note-5) When combining the Allegheny Province with the Kanawa Province, they run 400 miles (640 km).

The USGS physiographic classification of all land in the United States lists the Allegheny Mountains as a section within the larger [Appalachian Plateau](/wiki/Appalachian_Plateau "Appalachian Plateau") province.[[5]](#cite_note-6) It may be generally defined to the south by the [Allegheny Front](/wiki/Allegheny_Front "Allegheny Front"), and to the east by the [Susquehanna River](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") valley. To the west, the Alleghenies grade down into the dissected Allegheny Plateau. The westernmost ridges are considered to be the [Laurel Highlands](/wiki/Laurel_Highlands "Laurel Highlands") and [Chestnut Ridge](/wiki/Chestnut_Ridge_(Laurel_Highlands) "Chestnut Ridge (Laurel Highlands)") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), and [Laurel Mountain](/wiki/Laurel_Mountain_(West_Virginia) "Laurel Mountain (West Virginia)") and Rich Mountain in [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia").

Big Stone Ridge marks the southern extent of the Alleghenies and is an outlier of [Flat Top Mountain](/wiki/Flat_Top_Mountain_(West_Virginia) "Flat Top Mountain (West Virginia)"), with the [Tug Fork](/wiki/Tug_Fork "Tug Fork") river running along its western flank.[[6]](#cite_note-7) The land to the south and to the west of the Alleghenies is the [Valley and Ridge](/wiki/Valley_and_Ridge "Valley and Ridge") physiographic province.

### Allegheny Front and Allegheny Highlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Allegheny Front and Allegheny Highlands")]

The eastern edge of the Alleghenies is marked by the [Allegheny Front](/wiki/Allegheny_Front "Allegheny Front"), which is also sometimes considered the eastern terminus of the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"). This great [escarpment](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment") roughly follows a portion of the [Eastern Continental Divide](/wiki/Eastern_Continental_Divide "Eastern Continental Divide") in this area. Several gorges and valleys drain the Alleghenies: to the east, [Smoke Hole Canyon](/wiki/Smoke_Hole_Canyon "Smoke Hole Canyon") ([South Branch Potomac River](/wiki/South_Branch_Potomac_River "South Branch Potomac River")), and to the west the [New River](/wiki/New_River_(Kanawha_River) "New River (Kanawha River)") Gorge and the [Blackwater](/wiki/Blackwater_Canyon "Blackwater Canyon") and [Cheat Canyons](/wiki/Cheat_Canyon "Cheat Canyon"). Thus, about half the precipitation falling on the Alleghenies makes its way west to the [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and half goes east to [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay") and the Atlantic seaboard.

The highest ridges of the Alleghenies are just west of the Front, with an east/west elevational change of up to 3,000 feet (910 m). Absolute elevations of the Allegheny Highlands reach nearly 5,000 feet (1,500 m), with the highest elevations in the southern part of the range. The highest point in the Allegheny Mountains is [Spruce Knob](/wiki/Spruce_Knob "Spruce Knob") (4,863 ft; 1,482 m), on [Spruce Mountain](/wiki/Spruce_Mountain_(West_Virginia) "Spruce Mountain (West Virginia)") in [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia"). Other notable Allegheny highpoints include [Thorny Flat](/wiki/Thorny_Flat "Thorny Flat") on [Cheat Mountain](/wiki/Cheat_Mountain "Cheat Mountain") (4,848 ft; 1,478 m), [Bald Knob](/wiki/Bald_Knob "Bald Knob") on [Back Allegheny Mountain](/wiki/Back_Allegheny_Mountain "Back Allegheny Mountain") (4,842 ft; 1,476 m), and [Mount Porte Crayon](/wiki/Mount_Porte_Crayon "Mount Porte Crayon") (4,770 ft; 1,450 m), all in West Virginia; [Dans Mountain](/wiki/Dans_Mountain "Dans Mountain") (2,898 ft; 883 m) in Maryland, [Backbone Mountain](/wiki/Backbone_Mountain "Backbone Mountain") (3,360 ft; 1,020 m), the highest point in Maryland; [Mount Davis](/wiki/Mount_Davis_(Pennsylvania) "Mount Davis (Pennsylvania)") (3,213 ft; 979 m), the highest point in Pennsylvania, and the second highest, [Blue Knob](/wiki/Blue_Knob_(Pennsylvania) "Blue Knob (Pennsylvania)") (3,146 ft; 959 m).

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development")]

The Alleghenies contain relatively few large urban centers. The four most populous cities in the region are, in descending order, [Altoona](/wiki/Altoona,_Pennsylvania "Altoona, Pennsylvania"), [State College](/wiki/State_College,_Pennsylvania "State College, Pennsylvania"), and [Johnstown](/wiki/Johnstown,_Pennsylvania "Johnstown, Pennsylvania") in Pennsylvania, and [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Maryland "Cumberland, Maryland") in Maryland.

In the 1970s and 1980s, the [Interstate Highway System](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") was extended into the northern portion of the Alleghenies. The region is now served by a network of federal expressways—[Interstates 80](/wiki/Interstate_80 "Interstate 80"), [70](/wiki/Interstate_70 "Interstate 70")/[76](/wiki/Interstate_76_(east) "Interstate 76 (east)") and [68](/wiki/Interstate_68 "Interstate 68"). [Interstate 64](/wiki/Interstate_64 "Interstate 64") traverses the southern extremity of the range, but the Central Alleghenies (the "High Alleghenies" of eastern West Virginia) have posed challenges for highway planners owing to the region's rugged terrain and environmental sensitivities (see [Corridor H](/wiki/Corridor_H "Corridor H")). The central Alleghenies continue to rely on a limited network of secondary highways, and the region maintains a lower population density compared to adjacent areas.

In the [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications "Telecommunications") field, a unique impediment to development in the central Allegheny region is the [United States National Radio Quiet Zone](/wiki/United_States_National_Radio_Quiet_Zone "United States National Radio Quiet Zone") (NRQZ), a large rectangle of land—about 13,000 square miles (34,000 km2)—that straddles the border area of Virginia and West Virginia. Created in 1958 by the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission"), the NRQZ restricts all omnidirectional and high-power radio transmissions, although cell phone service is allowed throughout much of the area.

### Protected areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Protected areas")]

Much of the [Monongahela](/wiki/Monongahela_National_Forest "Monongahela National Forest") (West Virginia), [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington_National_Forest "George Washington National Forest") (West Virginia, Virginia) and [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson_National_Forest "Jefferson National Forest") (Virginia) [National Forests](/wiki/United_States_National_Forest "United States National Forest") lie within the Allegheny Mountains. The Alleghenies also include a number of federally designated [wilderness areas](/wiki/Wilderness_areas "Wilderness areas"), such as the [Dolly Sods Wilderness](/wiki/Dolly_Sods_Wilderness "Dolly Sods Wilderness"), [Laurel Fork Wilderness](/wiki/Laurel_Fork_North_Wilderness "Laurel Fork North Wilderness"), and [Cranberry Wilderness](/wiki/Cranberry_Wilderness "Cranberry Wilderness") in West Virginia.

The mostly completed [Allegheny Trail](/wiki/Allegheny_Trail "Allegheny Trail"), a project of the West Virginia Scenic Trails Association since 1975, runs the length of the range within West Virginia. The northern terminus is at the [Mason–Dixon line](/wiki/Mason%E2%80%93Dixon_line "Mason–Dixon line") and the southern is at the West Virginia-Virginia border on [Peters Mountain](/wiki/Peters_Mountain "Peters Mountain").[[7]](#cite_note-8)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geology")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this section by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(December 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The bedrock of the Alleghenies is mostly [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") and metamorphosed sandstone, [quartzite](/wiki/Quartzite "Quartzite"), which is extremely resistant to weathering. Prominent beds of resistant [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(geology) "Conglomerate (geology)") can be found in some areas, such as the [Dolly Sods](/wiki/Dolly_Sods "Dolly Sods"). When it weathers, it leaves behind a pure white quartzite gravel. The rock layers of the Alleghenies were formed during the [Appalachian orogeny](/wiki/Appalachian_orogeny "Appalachian orogeny").

Because of intense freeze-thaw cycles in the higher Alleghenies, there is little native bedrock exposed in most areas. The ground surface usually rests on a massive jumble of sandstone rocks, with air space between them, that are gradually moving down-slope. The crest of the Allegheny Front is an exception, where high bluffs are often exposed.

[Mineral springs](/wiki/Mineral_springs "Mineral springs") in the High Alleghenies attracted Native Americans and 18th century white settlers and provided a modest incentive to the local economy. The spas developing around these geological features include celebrated resorts that continue to cater to an exclusive clientele, such as [The Greenbrier](/wiki/The_Greenbrier "The Greenbrier") ([White Sulphur Springs, West Virginia](/wiki/White_Sulphur_Springs,_West_Virginia "White Sulphur Springs, West Virginia"); hotel built 1858) and [The Homestead](/wiki/The_Homestead_(Hot_Springs,_Virginia) "The Homestead (Hot Springs, Virginia)") ([Hot Springs, Virginia](/wiki/Hot_Springs,_Virginia "Hot Springs, Virginia"); original lodge built 1766).

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ecology")]

### Flora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Flora")]

The High Alleghenies are noted for their forests of [red spruce](/wiki/Red_Spruce "Red Spruce"), [balsam fir](/wiki/Balsam_Fir "Balsam Fir"), and [mountain ash](/wiki/Sorbus "Sorbus"), trees typically found much farther north. Hardwood forests also include [yellow birch](/wiki/Yellow_birch "Yellow birch") (whose specific name *Betula alleghaniensis* refer to the mountain range), [sugar](/wiki/Sugar_maple "Sugar maple") and [red maple](/wiki/Red_maple "Red maple"), [eastern hemlock](/wiki/Eastern_hemlock "Eastern hemlock"), and [black cherry](/wiki/Black_cherry "Black cherry"). [American beech](/wiki/American_beech "American beech"), pine and hickory can also be found. The forests of the entire region are now almost all [second- or third-growth forests](/wiki/Secondary_forest "Secondary forest"), the original trees having been removed in the late 19th and (in West Virginia) early 20th centuries. The [wild onion](/wiki/Allium "Allium") known as the ramp (*[Allium tricoccum](/wiki/Allium_tricoccum "Allium tricoccum")*) is also present in the deeper forests.

Certain isolated areas in the High Alleghenies are well known for their open expanses of [sphagnum bogs](/wiki/Sphagnum_bog "Sphagnum bog") and [heath shrubs](/wiki/Heath_shrubs "Heath shrubs") (e.g., [Dolly Sods](/wiki/Dolly_Sods "Dolly Sods"), [Cranberry Glades](/wiki/Cranberry_Glades "Cranberry Glades")). Many plant communities are indeed similar to those of sea-level eastern Canada. But the [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") within the Alleghenies are remarkably varied. In recent decades, the many stages of [ecologic succession](/wiki/Ecologic_succession "Ecologic succession") throughout the area have made the region one of enduring interest to botanists.

### Fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Fauna")]

The larger [megafauna](/wiki/Megafauna "Megafauna") which once inhabited the High Alleghenies—[elk](/wiki/Elk "Elk"), [bison](/wiki/Bison "Bison"), [mountain lion](/wiki/Mountain_lion "Mountain lion")—were all exterminated during the 19th century. They survived longer in this area, however, than in other parts of the eastern United States. Naturalist [John James Audubon](/wiki/John_James_Audubon "John James Audubon") reported that by 1851 a few eastern elk (*Cervus canadensis canadiensis*) could still be found in the Alleghany Mountains but that by then they were virtually gone from the remainder of their range. Mammals in the Allegheny region today include [whitetail deer](/wiki/Whitetail_deer "Whitetail deer"), [chipmunk](/wiki/Chipmunk "Chipmunk"), [raccoon](/wiki/Raccoon "Raccoon"), [skunk](/wiki/Skunk "Skunk"), [groundhog](/wiki/Groundhog "Groundhog"), [opossum](/wiki/Opossum "Opossum"), [weasel](/wiki/Weasel "Weasel"), [field mouse](/wiki/Vole "Vole"), [flying squirrel](/wiki/Flying_squirrel "Flying squirrel"), [cottontail rabbit](/wiki/Cottontail_rabbit "Cottontail rabbit"), [gray foxes](/wiki/Gray_foxes "Gray foxes"), [red foxes](/wiki/Red_foxes "Red foxes"), [gray squirrels](/wiki/Eastern_gray_squirrel "Eastern gray squirrel"), red [squirrels](/wiki/Squirrels "Squirrels") and a cave [bat](/wiki/Bat "Bat"). [Bobcat](/wiki/Bobcat "Bobcat"), [snowshoe hare](/wiki/Snowshoe_hare "Snowshoe hare"), [wild boar](/wiki/Wild_boar "Wild boar") and [black bear](/wiki/American_black_bear "American black bear") and [coyote](/wiki/Coyote "Coyote") are also found in the forests and parks of the Alleghenies. [Mink](/wiki/Mink "Mink") and [beaver](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver") are much less often seen.

These mountains and plateau have over 20 species of reptiles represented as lizard, skink, turtle and snake. Some of the [icterid](/wiki/Icterid "Icterid") birds visit the mountains as well as the [hermit thrush](/wiki/Hermit_thrush "Hermit thrush") and [wood thrush](/wiki/Wood_thrush "Wood thrush"). North American migrant birds live throughout the mountains during the warmer seasons. Occasionally, [osprey](/wiki/Osprey "Osprey") and [eagles](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle") can be found nesting along the streams. The [hawks](/wiki/Hawk "Hawk") and [owls](/wiki/Owl "Owl") are the most common [birds of prey](/wiki/Birds_of_prey "Birds of prey").

The water habitats of the Alleghenies hold 24 families of fish. Amphibian species number about 21, among them [hellbenders](/wiki/Hellbender "Hellbender"), [lungless salamanders](/wiki/Lungless_salamander "Lungless salamander"), and various [toads](/wiki/Toad "Toad") and [frogs](/wiki/Frog "Frog"). The Alleghenies provide habitat for about 54 species of common [invertebrate](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate"). These include [Gastropoda](/wiki/Gastropoda "Gastropoda"), [slugs](/wiki/Slugs "Slugs"), [leech](/wiki/Leech "Leech"), [earthworms](/wiki/Earthworms "Earthworms") and [grub worm](/wiki/Grub_worm "Grub worm"). [Cave](/wiki/Cave "Cave") [crayfish](/wiki/Crayfish "Crayfish") (*[Cambarus nerterius](/wiki/Cambarus_nerterius "Cambarus nerterius")*) live alongside a little over seven dozen cave invertebrates.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

### Pre-contact Native Americans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Pre-contact Native Americans")]

The indigenous people inhabiting the Allegheny Mountains emerged from the [greater region's archaic and mound building cultures](/wiki/West_Virginia_Prehistory "West Virginia Prehistory"), particularly the [Adena](/wiki/Adena_culture "Adena culture") and [Eastern Woodland](/wiki/Eastern_Woodland "Eastern Woodland") peoples with a later [Hopewellian](/wiki/Hopewell_culture "Hopewell culture") influence. These [Late Middle Woodland culture](/wiki/Woodland_Period#Middle_Woodland_Period_(1_to_500_CE),_Swift_Creek_Culture "Woodland Period") people have been called the Montaine (c. AD 500 to 1000) culture.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11) Their neighbors, the woodland Buck Garden culture, lived in the western valleys of the central Allegheny range. The Montaine sites extend from the tributaries of the upper [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") region south to the New River tributaries. These also were influenced by the earlier [Armstrong culture](/wiki/Armstrong_culture "Armstrong culture") of the more southwestern portions northern sub-range of the [Ouasioto (Cumberland) Mountains](/wiki/Cumberland_Mountains "Cumberland Mountains") and by the more easterly Virginia Woodland people. The Late Woodland Montaine were less influenced by Hopewellian trade from Ohio, although similarly polished stone tools have been found among the Montaine sites in the [Tygart Valley](/wiki/Tygart_Valley_River "Tygart Valley River").[[11]](#cite_note-12) Small groups of Montaine people appear to have lingered much beyond their classically defined period in parts of the most mountainous valleys.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The watershed of the [Monongahela River](/wiki/Monongahela_River "Monongahela River") is within the northwestern Alleghenies, and it is from it that the [Monongahela](/wiki/Monongahela_tribe "Monongahela tribe") culture takes its name. The [Godwin-Portman](/w/index.php?title=Godwin-Portman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Godwin-Portman (page does not exist)") site (36AL39) located in [Allegheny County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Allegheny_County,_Pennsylvania "Allegheny County, Pennsylvania"), had a possible [Fort Ancient](/wiki/Fort_Ancient "Fort Ancient") (c. AD 850 to 1680) presence during the 15th century.[[13]](#cite_note-14) Washington Boro ceramics have been found on the Barton (18AG3) and Llewellyn (18AG26) sites in Maryland on the northeastern slopes of the late Susquehannock sequence. The early Monongahela (c. AD 900 to 1630) are called the Drew Tradition in Pennsylvania. According to archeologist Richard L. George: "I believe that some of the Monongahela were of Algonquin origin.... Other scholars have suggested that Iroquoian speakers were interacting with Late Monongahela people, and additional evidence is presented to confirm this. I conclude that the archaeologically conceived term, Monongahela, likely encompasses speakers of several languages, including Siouan."[[14]](#cite_note-15) According to Dr Maslowski of West Virginia in 2009: "The New River Drainage and upper Potomac represents the range of the Huffman Phase (Page) hunting and gathering area or when it is found in small amounts on village sites, trade ware or Page women being assimilated into another village (tribe)." Finally, according to Prof Potter of Virginia, they [the people represented by the Huffman Phase of Page pottery] had occupied the eastern slopes of the Alleghenies on the upper Potomac to the northern, lower [Shenandoah Valley](/wiki/Shenandoah_Valley "Shenandoah Valley") region before the AD 1300 Luray phase ([Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples")) peoples' "invasion". It is thought that these ancient Alleghenians were pushed from the classic Huffman Phase of the eastern slopes of the Alleghenies to the [Blue Ridge Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Ridge_Mountains "Blue Ridge Mountains") in western [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), which was eastern [Siouan](/wiki/Siouan "Siouan") territory.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Carte-de-L-AMERICQVE-1671-clip.png/250px-Carte-de-L-AMERICQVE-1671-clip.png)](/wiki/File:Carte-de-L-AMERICQVE-1671-clip.png)

Detail of a French map of 1671. The Alleghenies are in the lower center portion.

In 1669, [John Lederer](/wiki/John_Lederer "John Lederer") and members of his party became the first Europeans to crest the Blue Ridge Mountains and the first to see the Shenandoah Valley and the Allegheny Mountains beyond.

### Native Americans in the 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Native Americans in the 17th century")]

The proto-historic Alleghenies can be exampled by the earliest journals of the colonists. According to Batts and Fallows' September, 1671 Expedition, they found Mehetan Indians of Mountain "Cherokee-Iroquois" mix on the New River tributaries. This journal does not identify the "Salt Village", but, that the "Mehetan" were associated with these and today thought to be "[Monetons](/wiki/Monetons "Monetons")", Siouans. However, this journal does not identify the "Salt Village" below the [Kanawha Falls](/wiki/Glen_Ferris,_West_Virginia "Glen Ferris, West Virginia"), but, that simply the "Mehetan" were associated with these. He explained, below the "Salt Villages", a mass of hostile Indians had, implied, arrived and some believe these to be "Shanwans" of Vielles Expedition of 1692~94, ancient Shawnee. In 1669, John Lederer of Maryland for the Virginia Colony and the Tennessee Cherokee had visited the mouth of the Kanawha and reported no hostilities on the lower streams of the Alleghenies. The Mohetan representative through a Siouan translator explained to Mr Batts and Mr Fallon, Colonel [Abraham Woods](/wiki/Abraham_Wood "Abraham Wood") explorers 1671–72, that he (Moheton [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_United_States "Indigenous peoples of the United States")) could not say much about the people below the "Salt Village" because they (Mountain Cherokee) were not associated with them. The Mohetan was armed by this time of 1671 for the Mohetan Representative was given several pouches of ammunition for his and the other's weapons as a token of friendship. Somebody had already been trading within the central Alleghenies before the Virginians' historical record begins in the Allegheny Mountains. Some earlier scholars found evidence these Proto-historics were either [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercian "Cistercian") of Spanish [Ajacan](/wiki/Ajacan "Ajacan") Occuquan outpost on the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") or [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") and their [Kahnawake](/wiki/Kahnawake "Kahnawake") Praying Indians ([Mohawk](/wiki/Mohawk_nation "Mohawk nation")) on the [Riviere de la Ronceverte](/wiki/Greenbrier_River "Greenbrier River"). The "[Kanawha Madonna](/wiki/Kanawha_Madonna "Kanawha Madonna")" may date from this period or earlier. Where the New River breaks through Peters' Mountain—near Pearisburg, Virginia—the 1671 journal mentions the "Moketans had formerly lived".

According to a number of early 17th century maps, the Messawomeake or "Mincquas" (Dutch) occupied the northern Allegheny Mountains. The "Shatteras" (an ancient Tutelo) occupied the Ouasioto Mountains and the earliest term [Canaraguy](/wiki/Monetons#Conoy "Monetons") (Kanawhans otherwise Canawest[[15]](#cite_note-16)) on the 1671 French map occupied the southerly Alleghenies. They were associated with the Allegheny "Cherokee" and Eastern Siouan as trade-movers and canoe transporters. The Calicuas, an ancient most northern Cherokee, migrated or was pushed from the Central Ohio Valley onto the north eastern slopes of the Alleghenies of the ancient Messawomeake, Iroquois tradesmen to 1630s Kent Island, by 1710 maps. Sometime before 1712, the Canawest ("Kanawhans"-"Canallaway"-"Canaragay") had moved to the upper Potomac and made a Treaty with the newly established trading post of Fort Conolloway which would become a part of western Maryland during the 1740s.

### Trading posts and other settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Trading posts and other settlements")]

Prior to European exploration and settlement, [trails through the Alleghenies](/wiki/Great_Indian_Warpath "Great Indian Warpath") had been transited for many generations by American Indian tribes such as the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois"), [Shawnee](/wiki/Shawnee "Shawnee"), [Delaware](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), [Catawba](/wiki/Catawba_(tribe) "Catawba (tribe)") and others, for purposes of [trade](/wiki/Trade "Trade"), [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting") and, especially, [warfare](/wiki/Endemic_warfare "Endemic warfare").[[16]](#cite_note-17) Western Virginia "[Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee")" were reported at [Cherokee Falls](/wiki/Valley_Falls_State_Park "Valley Falls State Park"), today's [Valley Falls](/wiki/Valley_Falls_State_Park "Valley Falls State Park") of the [Tygart Valley](/wiki/Tygart_Valley_River "Tygart Valley River").[[17]](#cite_note-18) Indian trader Charles Poke's trading post dates from 1731 with the Calicuas of Cherokee Falls still in the region from the previous century.

The "London Scribes" (The Crown's taxation records) vaguely mentions the colonial Alleghenian location of only a few other early colonial trading locations. A general knowledge of these few outposts are more of traditional telling of some local people. However, an example is the "Van Metre" trading house mentioned in an earlier edition of the "Wonderful West Virginia Magazine" being on the South Branches of the upper reaches of the Potomac. Another very early trading house appears on a lower Greenbrier Valley map during the earlier decades of the 18th century.

As early as 1719, new arrivals from Europe began to cross the lower Susquehanna River and settle illegally in defiance of the Board of Property in Pennsylvania, on un-warranted land of the northeastern drainage rivers of the Allegheny Mountains. Several Indian Nations requested the removal of "Maryland Intruders".[[18]](#cite_note-19) Some of these moved onward as territory opened up beyond the Alleghenies.

The first permanent European settlers *west* of the Alleghenies have traditionally been considered to have been two New Englanders: Jacob Marlin and Stephen Sewell, who arrived in the [Greenbrier Valley](/wiki/Greenbrier_Valley "Greenbrier Valley") in 1749. They built a cabin together at what would become [Marlinton, West Virginia](/wiki/Marlinton,_West_Virginia "Marlinton, West Virginia"), but after disputing over religion, Sewell moved into a nearby hollowed-out sycamore tree. In 1751, surveyor John Lewis (father of [Andrew Lewis](/wiki/Andrew_Lewis_(soldier) "Andrew Lewis (soldier)")) discovered the pair. Sewell eventually settled on the eastern side of Sewell Mountain, near present-day [Rainelle, West Virginia](/wiki/Rainelle,_West_Virginia "Rainelle, West Virginia").[[19]](#cite_note-20) They may well have been the first to settle what was then called the "western waters"—*i.e.*, in the regions where streams flowed westward to the Gulf of Mexico rather than eastward to the Atlantic.

### First surveys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: First surveys")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Kitfry-1-.jpg/250px-Kitfry-1-.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kitfry-1-.jpg)

The *[Fry-Jefferson Map](/wiki/Fry-Jefferson_Map "Fry-Jefferson Map")* (1751) prominently features "The Allagany Ridge of Mountains".

Among the first whites to penetrate into the Allegheny Mountains were surveyors attempting to settle a dispute over the extent of lands belonging to either [Thomas Fairfax, 6th Lord Fairfax of Cameron](/wiki/Thomas_Fairfax,_6th_Lord_Fairfax_of_Cameron "Thomas Fairfax, 6th Lord Fairfax of Cameron") or to the English [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council"). An expedition of 1736 by [John Savage](/wiki/John_Savage_(surveyor) "John Savage (surveyor)") established the location of the source of the [North Branch Potomac River](/wiki/North_Branch_Potomac_River "North Branch Potomac River"). In March 1742, a frontiersman named John Howard—along with his son and others—had been commissioned by [Governor Gooch](/wiki/Governor_Gooch "Governor Gooch") to explore the southwest of Virginia as far as the Mississippi River. Following [Cedar Creek](/wiki/Cedar_Creek_(James_River) "Cedar Creek (James River)") through the [Natural Bridge](/wiki/Natural_Bridge_(Virginia) "Natural Bridge (Virginia)"), they floated in buffalo-skin boats down the [New](/wiki/New_River_(Kanawha_River) "New River (Kanawha River)"), [Coal](/wiki/Coal_River_(West_Virginia) "Coal River (West Virginia)"), [Kanawha](/wiki/Kanawha_River "Kanawha River"), and [Ohio Rivers](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") to the Mississippi. Although captured by the French before he reached [Natchez](/wiki/Natchez,_Mississippi "Natchez, Mississippi"), Howard was eventually released and (in 1745) was interviewed by Fairfax. Howard's description of the [South Branch Potomac River](/wiki/South_Branch_Potomac_River "South Branch Potomac River") resulted in the definite decision by Fairfax to secure his lands in the region.[[20]](#cite_note-21) An expedition under [Peter Jefferson](/wiki/Peter_Jefferson "Peter Jefferson") and [Thomas Lewis](/wiki/Thomas_Lewis_(Virginia_politician) "Thomas Lewis (Virginia politician)") in the following year emplaced the "[Fairfax Stone](/wiki/Fairfax_Stone "Fairfax Stone")" at the source of the North Branch and established a line of demarcation (the "[Fairfax Line](/wiki/Fairfax_Line "Fairfax Line")") extending from the stone south-east to the headwaters of the [Rappahannock River](/wiki/Rappahannock_River "Rappahannock River"). Lewis' journal of that expedition provides a valuable view of the Allegheny country before its settlement.[[21]](#cite_note-22) Jefferson and [Joshua Fry](/wiki/Joshua_Fry "Joshua Fry")'s "Fry-Jefferson Map" of 1751 accurately depicted the Alleghenies for the first time. In the following decades, pioneer settlers arrived in the Alleghenies, especially during Colonial Virginia's [Robert Dinwiddie](/wiki/Robert_Dinwiddie "Robert Dinwiddie") era (1751–1758). These included [squatters](/wiki/Squatters "Squatters") by the [Quit-rent](/wiki/Quit-rent "Quit-rent") Law. Some had preceded the official surveyors using a ["hack on the tree and field of corn"](/wiki/Tomahawk_right "Tomahawk right") marking land ownership approved by the Virginia Colonial Governor who had to be replaced with Governor [John Murray, 4th Earl of Dunmore](/wiki/John_Murray,_4th_Earl_of_Dunmore "John Murray, 4th Earl of Dunmore").

### First roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: First roads")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Lewis%27_1755_Map%2C_Library_of_Congress.jpg/250px-Lewis%27_1755_Map%2C_Library_of_Congress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lewis%27_1755_Map,_Library_of_Congress.jpg)

A 1775 map of the Allegheny Plateau and Mountain Range

Trans-Allegheny travel had been facilitated when a military trail—[Braddock Road](/wiki/Braddock_Road_(Route_40) "Braddock Road (Route 40)")—was blazed and opened by the [Ohio Company](/wiki/Ohio_Company "Ohio Company") in 1751. (It followed an earlier Indian and pioneer trail known as [Nemacolin's Path](/wiki/Nemacolin%27s_Path "Nemacolin's Path").) Braddock Road connected Cumberland, Maryland (the upper limit of navigation on the Potomac River) and the forks of the Ohio River (the future [Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pittsburgh,_Pennsylvania "Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania")). It received its name from the British leader of the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") (1754–1763), General [Edward Braddock](/wiki/Edward_Braddock "Edward Braddock"), who led the ill-fated [Braddock expedition](/wiki/Braddock_expedition "Braddock expedition") four years later.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

In addition to the war, hunting and trading with Indians were primary motivations for white movement across the mountains. Permanent white settlement of the northern Alleghenies was facilitated by the explorations and stories of such noted Marylanders as the Indian fighter and trader [Thomas Cresap](/wiki/Thomas_Cresap "Thomas Cresap") (1702–1790) and the backwoodsman and hunter [Meshach Browning](/wiki/Meshach_Browning "Meshach Browning") (1781–1859).[[23]](#cite_note-24) In the late 18th century, a massive migration to the Monongahela River basin took place over three main routes: along the old Braddock Road via [Winchester, Virginia](/wiki/Winchester,_Virginia "Winchester, Virginia"); through the [Shenandoah Valley](/wiki/Shenandoah_Valley "Shenandoah Valley") to the head of the [Cheat River](/wiki/Cheat_River "Cheat River") and from there to the Monongahela; and along the [Lincoln Highway](/wiki/Lincoln_Highway "Lincoln Highway") to [Ligonier, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Ligonier,_Pennsylvania "Ligonier, Pennsylvania"), and thence along [Jacob's Creek](/wiki/Jacobs_Creek_(Pennsylvania) "Jacobs Creek (Pennsylvania)") to the Monongahela. These immigrants were predominantly [Scotch-Irish](/wiki/Scotch-Irish_American "Scotch-Irish American"), [German](/wiki/German_American "German American"), and, to a lesser extent, [British](/wiki/British_American "British American") stock.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Braddock Road was superseded by the [Cumberland Road](/wiki/Cumberland_Road "Cumberland Road")—also called the National Road—one of the first major improved highways in the United States to be built by the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_Government_of_the_United_States "Federal Government of the United States"). Construction began in 1811 at Cumberland and the road reached [Wheeling, Virginia](/wiki/Wheeling,_Virginia "Wheeling, Virginia") (now West Virginia) on the Ohio River in 1818. Just to the south, the state-funded [Staunton and Parkersburg Turnpike](/wiki/Staunton_and_Parkersburg_Turnpike "Staunton and Parkersburg Turnpike") was constructed to provide a direct route for the settlements of the Shenandoah Valley to the Ohio River by way of the [Tygart Valley](/wiki/Tygart_Valley "Tygart Valley") and [Little Kanawha](/wiki/Little_Kanawha_River "Little Kanawha River") Rivers. Planned and approved in 1826 and completed in 1848, the Staunton and Parkersburg was maintained by fees (tolls) collected at [toll houses](/wiki/Toll_house "Toll house") placed at regular intervals.

### First railroads and canals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: First railroads and canals")]

Construction on the [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad") began at [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") in 1828; the B&O traversed the Alleghenies, changing the economy and society of the Mountains forever.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The B&O had reached [Martinsburg, (West) Virginia](/wiki/Martinsburg,_West_Virginia "Martinsburg, West Virginia"), by May 1842, [Hancock, (West) Virginia](/wiki/Hancock,_West_Virginia "Hancock, West Virginia"), by June, Cumberland, Maryland, on November 5, 1842, [Piedmont, (West) Virginia](/wiki/Piedmont,_West_Virginia "Piedmont, West Virginia"), on July 21, 1851, and [Fairmont, (West) Virginia](/wiki/Fairmont,_West_Virginia "Fairmont, West Virginia"), on June 22, 1852. (It finally reached its Ohio River terminus at [Wheeling, (West) Virginia](/wiki/Wheeling,_West_Virginia "Wheeling, West Virginia"), on January 1, 1853.)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Chesapeake and Ohio Canal](/wiki/Chesapeake_and_Ohio_Canal "Chesapeake and Ohio Canal")—also begun in 1828, but at [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Georgetown (Washington, D.C.)")—was also a public work of enormous economic and social significance for the Alleghenies. It approached [Hancock, Maryland](/wiki/Hancock,_Maryland "Hancock, Maryland"), by 1839. From the beginning, the B&O Railroad and the [Chesapeake and Ohio Canal](/wiki/Chesapeake_and_Ohio_Canal "Chesapeake and Ohio Canal") operated in bitter legal and commercial competition with one another as they vied for rights to the narrow strips of land along the Potomac.[[25]](#cite_note-26) When the Canal finally reached Cumberland in 1850, the Railroad had already arrived eight years before.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Debt-ridden, the Canal company dropped its plan to continue construction of the next 180 miles (290 km) of the Canal into the Ohio Valley.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The company had long realized—especially with the difficult experience of digging the [Paw Paw Tunnel](/wiki/Paw_Paw_Tunnel "Paw Paw Tunnel")—that the original plan of construction over the mountains and all the way down the [Youghiogheny River](/wiki/Youghiogheny_River "Youghiogheny River") to [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") was "wildly unrealistic".[[28]](#cite_note-29)

Public works financed at the state level were not lacking during this period. The [Main Line of Public Works](/wiki/Main_Line_of_Public_Works "Main Line of Public Works") was a transportation network project built between 1826 and 1834 by the [Commonwealth of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). It created a railroad and canal system across southern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") between [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), intended to transport freight (notably including [anthracite coal](/wiki/Anthracite "Anthracite")) and people with greater reliability, speed and capacity. The Main Line of Public Works included the [Philadelphia and Columbia Railroad](/wiki/Philadelphia_and_Columbia_Railroad "Philadelphia and Columbia Railroad"), the [Allegheny Portage Railroad](/wiki/Allegheny_Portage_Railroad "Allegheny Portage Railroad") and the [Pennsylvania Canal](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Canal "Pennsylvania Canal") system.

### Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Civil War")]

Lying astride the border separating the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") and [Confederacy](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), the Alleghenies were among the areas most directly affected by the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") (1861–1865). One of the earliest campaigns of the War was fought for control of the Staunton and Parkersburg Turnpike and for the access it provided to the B&O Railroad. The [Battle of Rich Mountain](/wiki/Battle_of_Rich_Mountain "Battle of Rich Mountain") (July 11, 1861) gave the Federals control of the turnpike, of Tygart's Valley, and of all of the territory of western Virginia to the north and west, including the railroad. (Union General [George McClellan](/wiki/George_McClellan "George McClellan")'s victory in this theater would ultimately bring him promotion to commander the [Army of the Potomac](/wiki/Army_of_the_Potomac "Army of the Potomac").) The Federals fortified at [Cheat Summit](/wiki/Cheat_Summit "Cheat Summit"), and the Confederates established strongholds at [Camp Bartow](/wiki/Camp_Bartow "Camp Bartow") and [Camp Allegheny](/wiki/Camp_Allegheny "Camp Allegheny"). Here they faced each other warily through the fall of 1861 and the following winter. General [Robert E. Lee](/wiki/Robert_E._Lee "Robert E. Lee")'s attempt to attack [Cheat Summit Fort](/wiki/Battle_of_Cheat_Mountain "Battle of Cheat Mountain") (September 12–15, 1861) and Federal attempts to attack Bartow and Allegheny, all failed to change the strategic stalemate. Finally, the harsh, high elevation winter achieved what the troops had failed to accomplish, and in the spring of 1862 both armies moved on down the pike to the [Battle of McDowell](/wiki/Battle_of_McDowell "Battle of McDowell") (May 8, 1862), and then on to fight what became General [Stonewall Jackson](/wiki/Stonewall_Jackson "Stonewall Jackson")'s [Shenandoah Valley Campaign](/wiki/Shenandoah_Valley_Campaign "Shenandoah Valley Campaign") (spring 1862). Two years later, much of this contested area (along with much else) became part of the new state of [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia"). The very rugged terrain of the Alleghenies was not at all amenable to a large-scale maneuver war and so the actions that the area witnessed for the remainder of the conflict were generally [guerrilla](/wiki/Guerrilla "Guerrilla") in nature.

### Coal and timber industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Coal and timber industries")]

With the further spread of the railroad networks in the 1890s and early 1900s, many new towns developed and thrived in the Alleghenies. The lumbering and coal industries that boomed in the wake of the railroads brought a measure of prosperity to the region, but most of the revenues flowed out of the mountains to the cities of the [eastern seaboard](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") where the [captains of industry](/wiki/Captains_of_industry "Captains of industry") were headquartered. This inequity created a bitter legacy that would last for generations and form the foundation of the [mountaineers' poverty](/wiki/Social_and_economic_stratification_in_Appalachia "Social and economic stratification in Appalachia") and the area's immense [environmental degradation](/wiki/Environmental_degradation "Environmental degradation").

The most momentous disaster to afflict the people of the Alleghenies was the [Johnstown Flood](/wiki/Johnstown_Flood "Johnstown Flood")—locally known as the "Great Flood of 1889"—which occurred on May 31 of that year after the [catastrophic failure](/wiki/Catastrophic_failure "Catastrophic failure") of the [South Fork Dam](/wiki/South_Fork_Dam "South Fork Dam") on the [Little Conemaugh River](/wiki/Little_Conemaugh_River "Little Conemaugh River") 14 miles (23 km) upstream of the town of [Johnstown, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Johnstown,_Pennsylvania "Johnstown, Pennsylvania"). The dam broke after several days of extremely heavy rainfall, unleashing 20 million tons of water (18 million cubic meters) from the reservoir known as [Lake Conemaugh](/wiki/Lake_Conemaugh "Lake Conemaugh"). (This body of water had been built as part of the [Main Line of Public Works](/wiki/Main_Line_of_Public_Works "Main Line of Public Works"), then abandoned.) With a flow rate that temporarily equalled that of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"),[[29]](#cite_note-Perkins-30) the flood killed 2,209 people[[30]](#cite_note-Gibson-31) and caused US$17 million of damage (about $425 million in 2012 dollars). The [American Red Cross](/wiki/American_Red_Cross "American Red Cross"), led by [Clara Barton](/wiki/Clara_Barton "Clara Barton") and with 50 volunteers, undertook a major [disaster relief](/wiki/Disaster_relief "Disaster relief") effort.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Support for victims came from all over the United States and 18 foreign countries. After the flood, survivors suffered a series of legal defeats in their attempts to recover damages from the dam's owners. Public indignation at that failure prompted the development in American law changing a [fault](/wiki/Fault_(legal) "Fault (legal)")-based regime to [strict liability](/wiki/Strict_liability "Strict liability").

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 20th century")]

In the 1920s and '30s, Allegheny highways were extensively paved to provide access for automobiles.

From the 1950s to 1992, the United States government maintained a top secret continuity program known as [Project Greek Island](/wiki/Project_Greek_Island "Project Greek Island") at [The Greenbrier](/wiki/The_Greenbrier "The Greenbrier") hotel in the Alleghenies of southern West Virginia.

In August 1963, at the [March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom](/wiki/March_on_Washington_for_Jobs_and_Freedom "March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom"), Dr. [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") referenced the Alleghenies—among several in an evocative list of mountains—in his famous "[I Have a Dream](/wiki/I_Have_a_Dream "I Have a Dream")" speech, when he said "Let freedom ring from the heightening Alleghenies of Pennsylvania!"[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Allegheny_Mountains&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 21st century")]

The [Flight 93 National Memorial](/wiki/Flight_93_National_Memorial "Flight 93 National Memorial") is located at the site of the crash of [United Airlines Flight 93](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_93 "United Airlines Flight 93")—which was [hijacked](/wiki/Aircraft_hijacking "Aircraft hijacking") in the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks")—in [Stonycreek Township, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Stonycreek_Township,_Somerset_County,_Pennsylvania "Stonycreek Township, Somerset County, Pennsylvania"), about 2 miles (3.2 km) north of [Shanksville](/wiki/Shanksville,_Pennsylvania "Shanksville, Pennsylvania"). The memorial honors the passengers and crew of Flight 93, who stopped [Al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") terrorists from reaching their intended target.
